Upgrading our economic models for an age of uncertainty","content":"Our monetary policy strategy, the tools we use and the impact they have. Upgrading our economic models for an age of uncertainty. The ECB is upgrading its economic modelling to cope with growing uncertainty in a time of war and energy shocks. This ECB Blog highlights the limitations of the current toolkit and looks at where modelling is now heading. Economists nevertheless try to find patterns in the ways households and firms make decisions and interact, and in how this all adds up to what we call “the economy”. These models are like small laboratories in which a simulated economy can be poked, prodded and shocked, and forecasts can be confronted with the data. Which is why models are crucial in central banking: economists at the ECB maintain and use macroeconomic models to prepare forecasts, scenario analyses and policy prescriptions.","score":0.9203972,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.kucoin.com/news/flash/ecb-upgrades-economic-models-projects-3-inflation-in-2026","title":"ECB Upgrades Economic Models, Projects 3% Inflation in 2026","content":"ECB staff now project 3.0% average inflation in 2026 and 0.8% GDP growth. A hub for EU affairs — events, publications and media from thousands of authoritative channels, for 15 years.","score":0.7797419,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eiCcoIaL97c","title":"BREAKING: ECB President Signals Economic Uncertainty After ...","content":"BREAKING: ECB President Signals Economic Uncertainty After ECB Policy Meeting in Frankfurt | AF14
DWS News
316000 subscribers

30 Apr 2026
WATCH LIVE NOW as European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde addressed reporters in Frankfurt following the latest Governing Council meeting, where policymakers assessed the economic outlook for the Eurozone.

The ECB maintained its current policy stance while warning that inflation risks remain elevated, driven by global energy shocks and ongoing geopolitical tensions. Lagarde highlighted that while the Euro area economy shows resilience, growth risks are increasingly tilted to the downside.

Key concerns include rising energy prices, weaker consumer confidence, global supply chain pressures, and uncertainty linked to the Middle East conflict. The ECB reiterated its commitment to a data-dependent approach, emphasizing that future interest rate decisions will depend on inflation trends and incoming economic data.

Lagarde also stressed that inflation remains above target in the short term, though medium-term expectations remain anchored near 2%. The central bank continues to monitor wage dynamics, financial conditions, and credit tightening across the Eurozone.

This press briefing comes at a crucial moment for European monetary policy as markets watch closely for signals on future rate cuts or further tightening.

Spring 2026 Economic Forecast shows slowdown in growth as energy shock drives up inflation. After reaching 1.5% in 2025, GDP growth in the EU is now projected to slow down to 1.1% in 2026—a downward revision of 0.3 percentage points from the Autumn 2025 Economic Forecast projection (1.4%). Growth projections for the euro area are similarly revised down, to 0.9% in 2026 and 1.2% in 2027, from 1.2% and 1.4% respectively. Inflation in the EU is expected to reach 3.1% in 2026—a full percentage point higher than previously forecast—easing again to 2.4% in 2027. In the euro area, inflation is also revised up to 3.0% in 2026 and to 2.3% in 2027, compared to the autumn projections of 1.9% and 2.0% respectively.","score":0.5465209,"raw_content":null}],"response_time":1.63,"request_id":"5cc1bd62-1f1d-4ec9-9e1f-69ebc7489b98"}
